g4_revenue_trend.description: Buffett wrote that through 1973 and 1974 WPC continued to do fine as a business while intrinsic value kept climbing.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: The newspaper and media franchise was not showing operating deterioration at the time Berkshire bought; the opportunity came from market panic, not business decay.

g7_moat.description: The Washington Post combined local monopoly economics, brand trust, and scarce media assets in a way that gave it durable pricing power with both readers and advertisers. Buffett's core observation was that the market value had fallen to about $100M even though knowledgeable observers would have valued the business at $400M-$500M.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — quality media asset, but not available at anything like the Washington Post discount.
PASS — a solid operator, but the local-monopoly economics and bargain valuation setup were not comparable.

buffett_action: Berkshire built its Washington Post stake in 1973 at no more than about one-fourth of business value.
actual_result: A historic multidecade winner and one of Buffett's clearest Graham-meets-quality triumphs.
key_insight: This was a wonderful business available at a Graham discount, a rare combination that Buffett never forgot.
